topic: A student discovers their teacher follows them on social media
raw_output:
[story 1]

The notification popped up, bright and insistent, right in the center of my phone screen. It was a direct message from Mr. Harrison. My stomach did a quick flip, a sudden, unwelcome jolt of alarm. I hadn't even opened his profile in weeks; he was just the stern, impeccably dressed history teacher who lectured us on the fall of empires. Now, a picture of him, slightly blurred, captioned with a cryptic quote about forgotten truths, was staring back at me. I slowly lowered the phone, my fingers trembling slightly as I stared at the screen, trying to reconcile the man in the classroom with the digital presence demanding my attention. A slow, confused frown creased my forehead. What on earth was going on?

[story 2]

I was scrolling through my feed, mindlessly flicking through updates from friends, when I saw it—a post from Professor Albright. It was a candid photo taken outside the university library, laughing with a group of students, and the tag beneath it was undeniably mine. My breath hitched. I froze, my thumb hovering over the screen. My heart started thumping against my ribs, a rapid, uneven rhythm. I looked up sharply, scanning the hallway, half-expecting someone to be watching. When I couldn't find anyone, I let out a small, involuntary sigh, the sound catching in my throat. I tapped the screen again, just to confirm it wasn't a glitch, but the feeling of being intensely observed settled heavy in my chest.

[story 3]

It happened during a late-night study session. I was reviewing notes, the silence of the dorm room amplifying the sudden, sharp *ping* of a social media alert. It was Dr. Evans. I blinked several times, rereading the text. He had tagged me in a story—a very casual snapshot of his evening coffee ritual. My hand instinctively went to my mouth, muffling a sound that was somewhere between a gasp and a choked laugh. I pushed my chair back slightly, the scrape of metal on the floor sounding deafening in the quiet room. I stared at the screen, my expression unreadable, a strange mixture of surprise and utter disorientation washing over my features. I felt suddenly very exposed.
